subject: Physical Education and Sport

topics.introduction: Fitness goes beyond physical abilities like sit-ups or running. It's about the body's systems working efficiently to meet every day's demands and adapt to stress. Fitness connects closely to developing overall well-being. It includes physical, social, emotional, spiritual, and mental aspects that enable a learner to participate in various activities and sports effectively.

sub_topics.title: Health-Related Components of Fitness

instructional_blocks.description: Identifying health-related activities (Endurance, body composition, Flexibility, muscular strength…)
instructional_blocks.hook: What role do you think fitness activities play in maintaining a healthy lifestyle and how can you incorporate Physical Education into your daily routine?
teacher_steps: Guide learners to discuss health related fitness activities in groups
Consider the table of components and activities to help learners identify physical activities
learner_steps: In groups identify and discuss health related fitness activities

instructional_blocks.description: Discussing the benefits of health-related activities
teacher_steps: Facilitate group discussions on benefits
Explain additional benefits for comprehension
learner_steps: In groups, discuss the benefits of health-related activities
Share findings with the class

instructional_blocks.description: Performing various health related activities
teacher_steps: Demonstrate The Cooper 12-minute run test
Demonstrate Kasch-Boyer step test
Guide learners through flexibility tests
Allow learners to perform according to their abilities
learner_steps: Perform The Cooper 12-minute run test
Perform Kasch-Boyer step test
Perform Sit and Reach test
Perform Arm and Shoulder reach
Perform Trunk extension test

sub_topics.short_notes:
Health Related Fitness refer to the aspects of physical fitness that is directly related to an individual's overall health and well-being. An athlete should maintain good health to perform activities with ease and still have sufficient energy to handle forthcoming activities.

Components of Health-Related Fitness:

1. Cardiovascular Endurance: The ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply oxygen to the muscles during sustained physical activity. Activities: Walking, jogging, aerobics, sprint-jog-sprint, shuttle runs, swimming, cycling.

2. Muscular Strength: The ability of muscles to generate force against resistance. Activities: Lifting, pushing, tag of war, arm curls.

3. Muscular Endurance: The ability of muscles to sustain activity over time. Activities: Running, swimming, jogging, sit ups, pushups.

4. Flexibility: The range of motion in joints and muscles. Activities: Sit and reach, back extension, body sideways.

5. Body Composition: The proportion of body fat to lean body mass. Activities: Walking, running, jogging, cycling, skipping, sit ups.

Benefits of Health-Related Activities:

Physical Health:
- Improved Cardiovascular Health
- Enhanced Strength and Endurance
- Better Weight Management
- Increased Flexibility and Balance
- Boosted Immune System
- Enhanced Bone and Joint Health
- Higher Energy Levels

Mental and Emotional Benefits:
- Reduced Stress and Anxiety
- Improved Mood
- Enhanced Cognitive Function
- Better Sleep

Health Promotion:
- Encourages a Healthy Lifestyle
- Prevents Chronic Illnesses
- Supports Community Health
- Strengthens Mental Resilience
- Boosts Productivity and Energy

Disease Prevention:
- Lower Risk of Chronic Illnesses
- Strengthened Immune System
- Healthy Weight Management
- Improved Mental Health
- Enhanced Bone and Joint Health
- Prevention of Respiratory Problems

vocabulary.definition: Physical activity that raises the heart rate and improves cardiovascular health
The proportion of body fat to lean body mass
The ability of the heart, lungs, and blood vessels to supply oxygen to the muscles during prolonged physical activity
The range of motion in joints and muscles
The ability of muscles to sustain activity over time
The ability of muscles to exert force against resistance
The ability to perform physical activity with efficiency and effectiveness

criteria: Multiple-choice quizzes: Accuracy, time management, understanding of concepts
Essay questions: Content and relevance, organization, depth of analysis, examples and support, language and mechanics
Group project, Research and Field trip reports: Collaboration and contribution, creativity and initiative, presentation
Class participation and engagement (Debates): Preparation, active participation, critical thinking, communication skills, respect and collaboration

sub_topics.title: Isotonic activities

instructional_blocks.description: Describing the characteristics of isotonic activities
teacher_steps: Guide learners to describe characteristics in groups
learner_steps: Describe the characteristics of isotonic activities (Muscle contraction, joint movement, constant tension, dynamic movement)

instructional_blocks.description: Explaining the benefits of isotonic activities
teacher_steps: Facilitate explanation of benefits
Provide additional benefits for comprehension
learner_steps: Explain the benefits of isotonic activities individually and in small groups

instructional_blocks.description: Performing various isotonic exercises
teacher_steps: Demonstrate isotonic exercises
Allow learners to perform different isotonic exercises
Include other isotonic activities for learners to perform
learner_steps: Perform various isotonic exercises in smaller groups
Practice push-ups
Practice biceps/dumbbell/arm curls
Practice walking and climbing stairs

sub_topics.short_notes:
Isotonic activities are a type of exercise that involves muscle contractions and movements, where the muscle length and tension change simultaneously. In other words, isotonic activities require the muscles to contract and move the joints through a range of motion, while maintaining constant tension.

Characteristics of Isotonic Activities:

1. Muscle Contraction: Isotonic activities involve the contraction of muscles, which generates force to move the joints.

2. Movement: Isotonic activities require movement of the joints, which involves the lengthening and shortening of muscles.

3. Constant Tension: Isotonic activities maintain constant tension in the muscles throughout the range of motion.

4. Dynamic movement: Isotonic activities involve exercises that deal with active, controlled motion through a full range of joint movement with muscles contracting and lengthening against resistance.

Benefits of Isotonic Activities:

- Improved muscular strength and endurance: Isotonic exercises help build muscular strength and endurance by challenging muscles to work against resistance.

- Improved joint mobility and flexibility: Isotonic exercises can help improve joint mobility and flexibility by increasing range of motion and reducing stiffness.

- Improved Body Composition: Isotonic exercises can help reduce body fat percentage and increase lean muscle mass, leading to improved body composition.

- Improved Mental Health and Mood: Isotonic exercises can release endorphins, also known as 'feel-good' hormones, which can help improve mental health and mood.

- Better Sleep Quality: Regular isotonic exercise can help improve sleep quality and duration by reducing stress and anxiety.

Examples of Isotonic Exercises:
- Push-ups
- Biceps/dumbbell/arm curls
- Walking
- Climbing stairs

vocabulary.definition: occurs if a muscle changes length while generating force resulting in movement at a joint
The muscle shortens as it contracts (e.g., lifting a weight)
The muscle lengthens as it contracts (e.g., lowering a weight)
Movement that involves the contraction and relaxation of muscles to move the joints
The process by which muscles generate force to move the joints

sub_topics.title: Isometric Activities

instructional_blocks.description: Describing the characteristics of isometric activities
teacher_steps: Guide learners to describe characteristics in pairs and as a class
learner_steps: Describe the characteristics of isometric activities in pairs and as a class (Muscle contraction, no joint movement, constant tension, static movement)

instructional_blocks.description: Explaining the benefits of isometric activities
teacher_steps: Facilitate group explanations
Provide detailed benefits for learner understanding
learner_steps: In groups explain the benefits of isometric activities (Injury rehabilitation, muscle endurance…)

instructional_blocks.description: Performing various isometric activities
teacher_steps: Demonstrate different isometric exercises
Allow learners to perform various exercises
Guide learners through wall sits, planks, glute bridges
learner_steps: Perform various isometric exercises during a practical lesson
Practice pushing the wall
Practice wall sit
Practice plank
Practice glute bridge
Practice crouch position

sub_topics.short_notes:
Isometric activities, also known as isometric exercises or isometrics, are a type of physical activity where the muscles contract and generate force without moving the joints. In other words, the muscle length and joint angle remain constant, while the muscle tension changes.

Characteristics of Isometric Activities:

1. No Joint Movement: In isometric activities, the joints do not move or change position during the exercise. The joint angle remains constant, and there is no visible movement of the joint.

2. Muscle Contraction: Isometric activities involve the contraction of muscles. The muscle fibers shorten and then maintain a constant length.

3. Constant Muscle Length: During isometric activities, the muscle length remains constant. The muscles contract, but the length of the muscle does not change.

4. Increased Muscle Tension: Isometric activities involve an increase in muscle tension. The muscles contract and generate force, which increases muscle tension.

5. Static Contraction: Isometric activities involve static contractions, where the muscle contracts and then holds the contraction for a period.

Benefits of Isometric Activities:

- Increased Strength: Isometric exercises help build strength without moving your joints, which can be especially beneficial for people with joint problems or injuries.

- Improved Muscle Endurance: Isometric activities enhance muscle endurance, allowing you to sustain contractions for longer periods.

- Injury Rehabilitation: Isometric exercises are often used in physical therapy to help recover injuries, especially those affecting the joints.

Examples of Isometric Exercises:
- Pushing the wall
- Wall Sit
- Plank
- Glute Bridge
- Crouch position
- Scrum

vocabulary.definition: occurs when a muscle generates force without changing its length resulting in no movement at a joint
where the muscle contracts and then holds the contraction for a period

topics.introduction: Gymnastics are sports involving the performance of exercises requiring strength, flexibility, balance, coordination, agility endurance and control. The activities form an important part in Physical Education and make a significant contribution to the set goals. Gymnastics involves systematic and rhythmic exercises on the floor (ground), bars and apparatus.

sub_topics.title: Stunts and Tumbling activities

instructional_blocks.description: Discussing the importance of gymnastics
teacher_steps: Facilitate group discussions on importance of gymnastics
Guide learners to understand flexibility, endurance, agility benefits
learner_steps: In smaller groups discuss the importance of gymnastics

instructional_blocks.description: Applying safety rules of gymnastic activities
teacher_steps: Explain safety rules
Demonstrate proper warm-up and stretching
Show proper spotting techniques
learner_steps: Apply safety rules of gymnastic activities (Spotting, tuck-in your chin for all rolls, inspecting equipment, follow instructions…)

instructional_blocks.description: Performing tumbling and stunts activities
teacher_steps: Demonstrate tumbling and stunts activities
Guide learners through forward rolls, backward rolls, cartwheels
Ensure safety during all activities
learner_steps: In smaller groups perform a variety of stunts and tumbling activities
Practice forward roll
Practice backward roll
Practice cartwheel
Practice bridge
Practice astride vault
Practice hand stand with support

sub_topics.short_notes:
Stunts and tumbling activities refer to the skills performed that often involve lifting or supporting another gymnast and acrobatic movements performed on the ground or a tumbling track respectively.

Importance of Gymnastics:

Physical fitness: Gymnastics help improve overall physical fitness including strength, flexibility, coordination and endurance.

Body awareness: Through tumbling, gymnasts develop a better understanding of their body's movements in space. This body awareness is important for carrying out difficult routines safely and effectively.

Balance and control: Improves balance and control, which help prevent injuries and improve performance.

Building strength: Stunts help develop arms and legs which are essential for overall gymnastics performance.

Enhancing coordination: Performing stunts helps learners to coordinate their movements.

Encouraging fun: Participating in stunts can make practice enjoyable, keeping learners motivated and excited about gymnastics.

Agility: Gymnastics help to control body movement in space with speed and accuracy.

Flexibility: Performing gymnastic activities will help an individual to execute skills more effectively and maintain proper form and body positioning.

Safety Rules:

1. Warm-up and stretching: Always start with a proper warm-up to prepare the body and prevent injuries.

2. Use proper equipment: Ensure that all equipment is in good condition and suitable for the skill level of the gymnast.

3. Know your limits: Gymnasts should only attempt skills they are trained for and comfortable with.

4. Proper attire: Wear appropriate clothing that allows for movement and does not pose a risk of entanglement.

5. Safe landing: Learn how to fall safely to minimize injury risk.

6. Spotting: When learning new skills, a coach or partner should spot to provide support and reduce the risk of falls.

Examples of Tumbling Activities:
- Forward roll
- Astride vault
- Backward roll
- Hand stand with support
- Head stand

Examples of Stunts Activities:
- Forward roll
- Backward roll
- Cartwheel
- Bridge
- Angel rise
- Shoulder-knee balance

vocabulary.definition: Ability to move quickly and with accuracy
Ability to sustain prolonged physical activity or effort over an extended period of time
Range of motion in a person's joints and muscles
Sports involving the performance of exercises requiring strength, flexibility, balance and control
To move with repeated turning or rotating motion
Practice of assisting a gymnast during a skill or routine to ensure their safety
Skills or acrobatic routines that are performed on various apparatus
Acrobatic movements performed on the ground or a tumbling track
To leap or spring over something, especially by pushing on it with the hands or using a pole

topics.introduction: Recreational games are a structured form of play, usually undertaken for enjoyment, relaxation, and sometimes competition. They can be physical, mental, or a combination of both, and are always played according to specific rules. These activities play a vital role in promoting physical fitness, mental well-being, and social development.

sub_topics.title: Aerobics
competences: 1.7.1.1 Perform step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics

instructional_blocks.description: Demonstrating step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics
teacher_steps: Guide learners to explore various sources of rhythmic accompaniment
Explain characteristics of rhythmic background (Tempo, Beat, Meter)
Demonstrate rhythmic patterns
learner_steps: In groups, explore various sources of rhythmic accompaniment, their unique characteristics, and the features of rhythmic backgrounds
Perform various rhythmic patterns of aerobics while applying the three fundamentals of rhythm
Practice locomotor movements (walking, running, jumping, hopping, skipping, leaping)
Practice non-locomotor movements (bending, stretching, twisting, swaying, balancing)
Practice manipulative movements (throwing, catching, kicking, dribbling, striking)

sub_topics.short_notes:
Aerobic dances refer to rhythmic body movements with or without music following a certain rhythmic pattern. Aerobics can be non-locomotor or locomotor, and can be done individually, in pairs or in groups. They can be accompanied by music or routine counts and claps.

Sources of Rhythmic Accompaniment:
- Dance drum
- Record player
- C.D. player
- Cassette player
- Piano
- Tape recorder
- Tambourine
- Rhythm bands
- Guitar
- Clapping or stamping
- Rattles

Characteristics of Rhythmic Background:

1. Tempo: This is the speed of the music. It can be constant or show a gradual increase or decrease.

2. Beat: This is the underlying rhythm of music. Sometimes it is referred to as the pulse of the music. Music with pronounced beat is easier to follow.

3. Meter: This refers to the manner in which the beats are put together to form a measure of music. It is typically noted at the beginning of a piece with a time signature which consists of two numbers.

Types of Rhythmic Movements:

1. Locomotor movements: These are rhythmic movements that enable an individual to move from one place to another. Examples include walking, running, jumping, hopping, skipping and leaping.

2. Non-locomotor movements: These are rhythmic movements that are performed without changing location, only body parts are the ones that move. Examples include bending, stretching, twisting, swaying and balancing.

3. Manipulative movements: These movements involve the control and movement of objects using the body or handling objects. Examples include actions like throwing, catching, kicking dribbling and striking.

vocabulary.definition: Exercises that combine rhythmic activities with stretching and strength training routines
Underlying rhythm of music
Rhythmic structure of music
Speed pace at which a piece of music is played or performed
pattern of sounds and silences in music or speech

assessment.expected_standard: Step and rhythmic patterns of aerobics performed accurately

sub_topics.title: Traditional games

instructional_blocks.description: Performing traditional games
teacher_steps: Guide learners to research indigenous Zambian traditional games
Facilitate performance of various game categories
learner_steps: Research and perform the indigenous Zambian Traditional games that are practiced or played in their various communities
Perform chasing games (Chain tag game…)
Perform breakthrough games (Sheep-sheep come all…)
Perform rhythmic games (Musical Chairs…)
Perform knowledge testing games (Who Am I?...)
Perform manipulative and calculative games

instructional_blocks.description: Highlighting the importance of Zambian Traditional Games
teacher_steps: Facilitate group discussions on importance
Explain cultural, social, physical, mental, and emotional benefits
learner_steps: In smaller groups discuss the importance of Zambian Traditional Games

sub_topics.short_notes:
Traditional games are activities passed down through generations, associated with specific ethnic groups or societies. They often feature simple rules, require minimal equipment, and have unknown origins or discoverers.

Importance of Zambian Traditional Games:

1. Preservation of Cultural Heritage:
- Traditional games are a way of passing down customs, values and stories from one generation to another
- They reflect Zambia's rich ethnic diversity and promote pride in indigenous identities
- They promote, preserve and maintain cultural values in society
- They consolidate historical background of cultures
- They provide and promote acceptance and appreciation of other cultures from different backgrounds and regions

2. Social Development:
- Most games are played in groups, promoting teamwork, cooperation, and communication
- Learners acquire important social skills like turn-taking, fairness, leadership, and conflict resolution
- They prevent anti-social behavior and vandalism
- They are a source of enjoyment and fun
- They act as an agent of socialization in the society
- They build adolescence belief in their ability to influence or resist their peers

3. Physical Development:
- Traditional games involving running, jumping, chasing, or balancing help improve motor skills, physical fitness, and coordination
- They encourage learners to stay active and healthy through play

4. Mental and Cognitive Growth:
- Traditional games stimulate critical thinking, problem-solving, memory and numeracy
- Knowledge-based games enhance language development and creativity
- They help promote self-esteem and confidence in oneself

5. Emotional Well-being:
- Participating in traditional games boosts confidence and self-esteem as learners learn to win and lose gracefully
- Traditional games provide a healthy outlet for releasing energy and emotions, reducing stress and anxiety
- They help to teach good attitudes to learners

6. Education through Informal Learning:
- Traditional games naturally integrate learning into play, making it easier for learners to grasp academic and life concepts
- Concepts like counting, strategy, and rhythm are learned in a fun and engaging way
- They act as informal education process

7. Inclusion and Accessibility:
- Traditional games require minimal or no equipment, making them accessible to learners in both urban and rural areas
- They often encourage inclusivity, allowing learners of different ages and abilities to participate

8. Community Bonding:
- During festivals or communal gatherings, traditional games bring people together across generations, reinforcing a sense of belonging and unity
- Traditional games help shape children into responsible and respectful adults by teaching important values such as honesty, patience, perseverance, discipline and respect for others

vocabulary.definition: Shared beliefs, values, customs and behaviors of a group of people
Any activity or sport that involves structured play, often with rules and objectives
Activities that are done for enjoyment, relaxation and leisure
Specific guidelines that dictate how a game is played

topics.introduction: Swimming refers to the act of moving through water using coordinated movements of the arms and legs typically employing specific strokes. In modern times, swimming is widely recognized for its many benefits. It provides a full-body workout, improving cardiovascular endurance, muscle strength, flexibility and coordination.

sub_topics.title: Water Safety

instructional_blocks.description: Creating simulated water safety scenario
teacher_steps: Give roles to learners (Lifeguard, Swimmer in distress, Onlookers, Emergency responders)
Guide the scenario step-by-step
Pause at key moments to ask questions
Encourage reflection and improvement
learner_steps: Simulate a pool emergency scenario
Set up the scene with towels outlining a pool area
Take up assigned roles
Execute scenario: Lifeguard observes and responds, Swimmer acts distressed, Onlookers react, Emergency responders provide assistance
Demonstrate proper rescue techniques without entering water unnecessarily
Practice CPR if needed

instructional_blocks.description: Researching and presenting about different water safety topics
teacher_steps: Assign topics to groups
Guide research on rip currents, pool safety, water-borne illnesses
Facilitate presentations
learner_steps: In small groups, conduct research on assigned water safety topics
Identify key facts, risks and prevention strategies
Create visual aids (Poster/slideshow)
Prepare and deliver brief presentations

instructional_blocks.description: Demonstrating water safety practices
teacher_steps: Set-up depth quiz with colored markers
Guide audit of school swimming pool
Demonstrate life-saving skills
Practice CPR techniques
learner_steps: Practice depth of pool awareness
Match activities to correct zones (shallow/deep)
Conduct audit of school swimming pool facilities
Write report on safety improvements needed
Practice life-saving skills: Reach, Throw, Don't Go methods
Perform CPR practice on training models
Practice passive drowning response rescue

sub_topics.short_notes:
Water safety refers to the practices, precautions and awareness needed to prevent accidents, injuries or drowning in aquatic environments. Participating in water activities requires personal responsibility, respect for safety guidelines and an understanding of water conditions.

Key Water Safety Topics:

1. Rip Currents:
- These are powerful, fast-moving channels of water that flow from the shore to deep water
- They appear as darker, uneven water with a noticeable difference in wave pattern
- They often pull swimmers away from the shore because they flow from the shore toward deeper water in a narrow fast-moving current

2. Pool Safety:
- Use pool covers when the pool is not in use to prevent accidental falls or drownings
- Limit the use of alcohol or drugs while swimming
- All individuals, especially children at an early age must learn to swim to increase safety in the water
- Know how to administer Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation
- Regularly keep the pool clean and well maintained to prevent accidents
- Shallow and deep end safety must be marked clearly so as to prevent swimmers jumping or diving in shallow water

3. Waterborne Illnesses:
- These are diseases caused by harmful microorganisms (bacteria, viruses and parasites) found in contaminated water which can lead to Bilharzia, typhoid, dysentery, cholera…
- These can occur when people swim, drink or come into contact with contaminated water, especially in pools, lakes, rivers or even oceans

Water Safety Practices:

1. Pool Depth Awareness:
- Identify shallow and deep ends using tiles, signs or ropes
- Practice entering feet first in shallow water and using ladders or steps
- Test floating in different depths to understand how buoyancy varies

2. Life-Saving Skills:
- Rescue scenarios: Practice 'Reach, Throw, Don't Go' methods using poles, ropes or floatation devices
- Cardio-Pulmonary Resuscitation (CPR) Practices: Perform chest compressions and rescue breathing
- Passive drowning response: Role-play rescuing an unconscious swimmer, securing them face-up and towing them to safety

vocabulary.definition: anything related to water
the force that allows objects to float in a liquid like water
A lifesaving technique to restore blood circulation and breathing in someone who has stopped breathing or whose heart has stopped
Being aware and able to think about one's surroundings, thoughts, and emotions
Tiny living organisms often too small to be seen with the naked eye
A person who saves someone from a dangerous or harmful situation
Powerful, fast-moving waves of water that flow from the shore to deeper waters
An imagined or planned situation that describes a particular setting
Diseases caused by harmful microorganisms in contaminated water

sub_topics.title: Pool

instructional_blocks.description: Practicing Pool Hygiene
teacher_steps: Guide learners to identify good pool practices
Facilitate poster design highlighting key hygiene rules
Display posters as appropriate
learner_steps: In smaller groups identify good pool practices
Design posters highlighting key rules on pool hygiene
Display posters as guided by the teacher

instructional_blocks.description: Debating Pool Hygiene Practices
teacher_steps: Provide motions for debate
Guide learners with different motions to appreciate pool hygiene practices
Facilitate structured debate
learner_steps: Debate for or against motions concerning pool hygiene practices
Motion 1: Public swimming pools should implement mandatory hygiene checks before opening each day
Motion 2: Recreational floatation devices should be banned in public swimming pools to improve water hygiene
Motion 3: Learners should be required to wear approved swimming costumes in public pools

sub_topics.short_notes:
A pool is a specially built area filled with water where people swim, play or learn water skills. To keep the pool safe and enjoyable for everyone, pool hygiene and good practices are very important.

Good Pool Practices:

1. Never swim immediately after a heavy meal. Wait at least 30 minutes after a light meal, or more after a heavy meal.

2. Always shower before swimming. For sanitary reasons it is very important for a person to take a shower before entering the pool. It is also advised to shower after swimming.

3. Always put on the correct swimming costume.

4. Water must always be kept clean by not spitting, urinating, or defecating in the water.

5. Not to chew gum or anything while swimming.

6. Not to eat or drink in the pool premises.

Pool Hygiene Importance:

Proper pool hygiene is essential for a healthy swimming environment. Maintaining good pool hygiene is a shared responsibility between pool owners and swimmers. Regular cleaning, chemical treatment and adhering to these practices help prevent the spread of waterborne diseases and ensure a safe and pleasant experience for all swimmers.

vocabulary.definition: Moving through water by using limbs or floating
Completely sinking something under the water's surface
